stories_covered: Anthropic raises $45 billion from hyperscalers, still compute-constrained
OpenAI's model performance lag confirmed by user growth and revenue misses
China blocks Meta's $2 billion Manus acquisition
Toma Bravo walks away from Medallia, handing $5.1 billion equity wipeout to creditors
Private equity model breaking as debt no longer serviceable even with modest leverage
harry_takes: Agents will choose what models and vendors to use, not humans—OpenAI's advantage over Anthropic is ephemeral as agent preferences shift weekly
Pre-AI SaaS companies face a three-bucket future: winners with AI (accelerating growth), systems of record (bounded cash), or melting icebergs (terminal decline)
Compute equals revenue is a stupid statement; compute plus great model equals revenue, but either alone is insufficient
The exit funnel is changing: IPOs only if you hit 400M+ ARR, PEs only at fire-sale prices, strategics only for narrow fit
Private equity overleverage didn't kill Medallia—overpayment in 2021 did, showing the danger of high multiples even with low debt
harry_boldest_quote: Your job as an investor is to tell you what something's worth four years from now.
predictions: Jason Lemkin: Agents will pick OpenAI for most workflows going forward, ending Anthropic's human-preference advantage
Panel: Only a handful of $1T+ companies can afford $100B+ acquisitions; Medallion/Medallia downgrades signal PE exit route collapsing
Rory: 60% solutions must trade at fair value or below, not narrative premium—venture upside dies without pixie dust
disagreements: Harry argued some value traps might still work at low multiples; Jason countered that low-growth SaaS can never re-accelerate enough to justify venture returns.
harry_role: Moderating between Rory's capital-allocation pessimism and Jason's binary agent-choice thesis, punctuating with framework-setting comments on the three buckets of SaaS futures.
notable: Jason's statement that agents, not humans, will choose LLMs marks a fundamental shift in competitive dynamics—the market cap arbitrage between model providers collapses once agents decouple from human preference.
